proposed_short: The vowel that marks a verb's conjugation class: a in -are, e in -ere, i in -ire. It links the stem to the endings.
proposed_long: Every Italian verb belongs to a conjugation identified by its theme vowel, the vowel of the infinitive ending: parlare is an a-verb, prendere an e-verb, dormire an i-verb. The theme vowel surfaces between the root and the personal endings across many tenses. In the simple future it is the centre of the action: the -are class shifts its theme vowel a to e (parlare gives parlerò, not parlarò), the -ere and -ire classes keep theirs (prenderò, dormirò), and the irregular syncopated verbs drop it altogether (avere gives avrò). Naming the theme vowel lets us describe all three behaviours, shift, keep, and drop, in one vocabulary.
rationale: Used throughout the future-formation explanations to describe the a-to-e shift, the e/i kept by -ere/-ire, and the vowel dropped by the syncopated set. The glossary has 'first/second/third conjugation' but no term for the vowel that distinguishes them and that the future manipulates.

proposed_short: Dropping an internal vowel of a word, as when avere loses its theme vowel to give the future stem avr- (avrò).
proposed_long: Syncopation is the loss of a sound, usually a vowel, from the middle of a word. A closed set of high-frequency Italian verbs syncopates in the future and conditional: the theme vowel drops, leaving the root directly before the -r- of the ending. So avere gives avr- (avrò), andare gives andr- (andrò), dovere gives dovr-, potere gives potr-, sapere gives sapr-, vedere gives vedr-. Because the endings stay regular, the syncopated stem is the whole of the irregularity. Keeping the vowel (averò, andero) is the characteristic learner slip. Syncopation also feeds the assimilated double-r stems (volere gives vorr-), where the cluster left behind further changes.
rationale: Used in every explanation for the irregular.syncopated leaf and referenced by the double-r explanations. The glossary has no term for the vowel-dropping that defines this group; 'stem change' is too generic.

proposed_short: When one sound changes to match a neighbour, as the cluster in volere's future stem becomes a doubled r: vorrò.
proposed_long: Assimilation is a sound becoming more like an adjacent sound. In the Italian future, a subgroup of the syncopated verbs goes one step further: once the theme vowel drops, the consonant left beside the -r- of the ending assimilates into a doubled r. So volere gives vorr- (vorrò), venire gives verr- (verrò), tenere gives terr-, rimanere gives rimarr-, bere gives berr-. The signature of the group is the -rr-, and writing a single r (vorò, verò) is the classic miss. Assimilation is why these verbs are grouped apart from the plain syncopated set like avrò, where no doubling occurs.
rationale: Used in the irregular.double_r explanations to name why the cluster becomes -rr-. The glossary has 'consonant doubling' (for spelling) but not the phonological process that produces the future double-r stems.

proposed_short: The compound future (futuro anteriore): the future of the auxiliary plus a past participle, as avrò finito 'I will have finished'.
proposed_long: The future perfect, called the futuro anteriore in Italian, expresses an action that will be complete before another point in the future: 'Quando arriverai, avrò gia finito' (when you arrive, I will have already finished). It is built from the future of the auxiliary (avere or essere) plus the past participle, exactly mirroring the passato prossimo but with the auxiliary in the future. The same auxiliary-choice and participle-agreement rules apply: motion and change-of-state verbs take essere and agree the participle with the subject (sara andata), while most others take avere with an invariable participle (avro finito). It also has a suppositional use about a completed past ('Sara gia partito', he has probably already left), which belongs to usage rather than formation.
rationale: Used in the future_anteriore explanations. The glossary has 'compound tense', 'passato prossimo', and 'trapassato prossimo' but no entry for this specific compound; 'compound future' alias keeps it findable. NOTE: the essere explanations also lean on 'suppletive', which PresentFormationAuthor already proposed in glossary_suggestions_verb_form.present_indicative.json (still pending merge); I have not re-proposed it here to avoid a duplicate.
